challenges.situation: You've been the go-to person for a core system for four years. A new technology emerges that could replace major parts of it. Your team asks what you think. You notice your first instinct is to list reasons it won't work here.

options.text: Trust your instinct — you know this system's constraints better than anyone, and most new tech is hype.
options.feedback: Your constraints knowledge is real. But 'it won't work here' in under a minute is the Einstellung effect — your proven solution blocking sight of alternatives. Nokia's engineers also knew their constraints better than anyone.

options.text: Say: 'My first reaction is skepticism — let me sit with that before I respond, because I might be defending my map.'
options.feedback: Naming the reflex breaks its power. This isn't weakness — it's the rare honesty of an expert who knows that their expertise is both their greatest asset and their greatest blind spot.

options.text: Delegate the evaluation to someone without your history — they'll be more objective.
options.feedback: Better than dismissing it, but abdication isn't the same as engagement. The team needs your depth AND your openness. Delegating entirely means your expertise doesn't contribute to the evaluation.

challenges.situation: A company that dominated its industry for 20 years hires consultants who recommend a fundamental strategy shift. The executive team finds fourteen reasons the consultants 'don't understand the business.'
challenges.question: What's the primary force keeping leadership from hearing the advice?

options.text: The competency trap — their success within the current model makes questioning the model feel like questioning their competence.
options.feedback: Twenty years of success creates twenty years of identity investment. The consultants aren't just suggesting a new strategy — they're implying that the thing these executives built their careers on might be ending. That's an identity threat.

options.text: Legitimate domain expertise — outsiders frequently underestimate industry-specific constraints.
options.feedback: Sometimes true. But fourteen reasons in a rapid response pattern is defense, not analysis. Genuine evaluation takes time and curiosity. Speed reveals the motive.

options.text: Organizational inertia — it's too complex to change course with so many dependencies.
options.feedback: Complexity is real, but 'too complex to change' is different from 'we refuse to see why change is needed.' Nokia's system was complex too — the complexity became the excuse, not the reason.

challenges.front: Nokia failed because they were complacent and stopped innovating.
challenges.back: Nokia had touchscreen prototypes before the iPhone. They had more R&D engineers than Apple. They weren't lazy — they were expert. Their failure was knowing too much about what phones were, which made it impossible to see what phones would become. Expertise didn't make them complacent. It made them blind in the exact direction they were looking.

challenges.context: An engineering lead has built the team's deployment pipeline over three years. A new platform emerges that could replace it entirely.

scenarioA.label: Nokia's response
scenarioA.text: Lists everything the new platform can't do that the current pipeline handles. Points out migration costs, learning curves, and edge cases. The evaluation ends before it begins.

scenarioB.label: Beginner's mind
scenarioB.text: Asks: 'If we were starting fresh today with no existing system, would we build what we have?' Runs a time-boxed spike on the new platform. Lets the answer emerge from evidence, not investment.

challenges.explanation: The question 'would we build this again from scratch?' bypasses the sunk cost of expertise. Nokia's engineers never asked it — they only asked 'what can this new thing NOT do that ours can?' which is a question designed to protect the past, not discover the future.

challenges.instruction: Order these from earliest warning sign of the expertise trap to point of no return:
items: You optimize within your current framework but never question the framework itself
You can't name an assumption in your domain that you've updated recently
You're the fastest person in the room at explaining why new approaches won't work
Your team stops bringing you disruptive ideas because they know what you'll say
correctOrder: You optimize within your current framework but never question the framework itself
You're the fastest person in the room at explaining why new approaches won't work
You can't name an assumption in your domain that you've updated recently
Your team stops bringing you disruptive ideas because they know what you'll say

principle.text: Expertise is a map, not the territory.
principle.subtext: The more detailed your map becomes, the more you trust it over your eyes — and the more catastrophic the failure when the territory shifts beneath you.

reflection: What's the equivalent of 'physical keyboards are better' in your domain — a belief that's currently true and widely held, but might be the thing that makes your expertise irrelevant in five years?
